topic: Membach SG null (1999 eclipse) — c3-patch verification + new arithmetic error
change_summary: detail_evidence drifted 28% (1961 → 2721) since c3 fingerprint, due to commit 159f0c6 (decider self-apply ISS-1527/1528/1529/1530) hardening WIN-013 against c3 holes. This c4 verifies whether the patches landed correctly and whether they introduced new issues.
our_argument_summary: WIN-013 remains classified Misleading. The dome claims a 0.0 μGal Membach SG null as confirmation, while WIN-011 claims a positive Mohe spring-gravimeter detection of the same physical eclipse. We argue the dome cannot credit both opposite outcomes without advance specification of which sites should respond. The c3 hardening added (a) sensitivity bounds (100–500× SG-vs-spring), (b) a subsurface-conductivity preemptive against the location-dependent escape, (c) a Van Camp 1999/2001 year-discrepancy parenthetical, and (d) a specialized post-hoc paragraph naming Van Camp et al. 2001 directly. Three of the four patches landed cleanly; the year-discrepancy patch (ISS-1529) introduced an arithmetic error that needs reverting.

dome_code_analysis.calculation_pipeline: WIN-013 still has no monitor.py validation — Domain 7 covers only WIN-011/012. The Membach 0.0 μGal value is editorial in 02-wins.txt with no fetch and no comparison. c3's 'monitoring=none' tag remains correct.
dome_code_analysis.hardcoded_vs_computed: Neither — no validation logic. Only narrative text in 02-wins.txt asserting Van Camp 1999/0.0 μGal/electromagnetic.
data_sources_used: None — monitor.py contains no fetch for WIN-013.
dome_code_analysis.escape_clauses: The dome's narrative reframe ('electromagnetic, not gravitational') is the entire escape mechanism. No code-level adaptive tolerance exists because there is no code-level check.
dome_code_analysis.code_discrepancies: None. The current code_analysis tag block (monitoring=none, relabels_standard=false, post_hoc=true, derives_from_dome=false, reviewed=true) accurately reflects monitor.py reality and matches the c3 corrections.

code_analysis_tags.monitoring_evidence: Re-grepped monitor.py and pull_data.py for 'membach', 'van camp', '013', and 'allais' — zero hits. Domain 7 in monitor.py covers only the WIN-011/012 coupling-constant cluster (κ = ΔB/Δg). No fetch, no comparison, no expected/observed pair for WIN-013. The 0.0 μGal value lives in 02-wins.txt narrative (line 90: 'Source: Van Camp 1999. 0.0 uGal. Confirms electromagnetic, not gravitational.') and never enters automated checking.
code_analysis_tags.relabels_standard_evidence: Standard physics predicts no eclipse-induced gravity anomaly at SG sensitivity. SG nulls are the expected outcome under standard physics; the dome is not relabeling a standard explanation here. It is post-hoc credit-claiming an expected null and re-framing it as 'EM, not gravitational' — which is a dome-specific narrative move (and self-contradictory with WIN-011/012), not a relabel.
code_analysis_tags.post_hoc_evidence: Van Camp et al.'s eclipse gravimetry result was published in 2001 (DOI 10.1023/A:1013940928637) — two years after the 1999 eclipse, and well before the dome model existed. The 0.0 μGal value was indexed and citable long before any dome 'prediction' commit. The dome cites 'Van Camp 1999' (the eclipse year) which obscures the publication-vs-eclipse timeline; our review now surfaces this discrepancy in the user-visible panel.
code_analysis_tags.derives_from_dome_evidence: No dome equation predicts Membach SGs should report 0.0 μGal. Disc radius, firmament height, n(r), and the V12 coordinate function play no role in producing the 0.0 number. The value is adopted from Van Camp 2001.

holes_found.description: **Arithmetic error introduced by ISS-1529 patch.** The new parenthetical in detail_evidence reads: "the dome cites 'Van Camp 1999' (the eclipse year); the paper appeared in 2001, **five years after the eclipse**, making the dome's 1999 attribution impossible to reconcile with publication history." 2001 − 1999 = 2 years, not 5. The figure of '5 years' likely came from conflating two distinct intervals: (i) eclipse-to-paper = 2 years, and (ii) paper-to-dome-model = roughly 5 years (the c3 review used '...published in 2001 — five years before the dome model existed' for the post-hoc argument, which is correct). The patch flattened those two intervals into one wrong number. A dome defender will catch this because it is a verifiable subtraction — it lets them say 'your reviewers can't even subtract' which is exactly the kind of cheap rhetorical win our credibility cannot afford. Note: detail_verdict_text retains the correct phrasing ('publicly indexed since 2001 — five years before the dome model existed'), so the inconsistency is internal: the same review uses '5 years' to mean two different things.
holes_found.recommendation: Replace 'five years after the eclipse' with 'two years after the eclipse' in detail_evidence. If the intent was to flag that the paper post-dates the dome model framing, separate the two intervals explicitly: 'the paper appeared in 2001, two years after the 1999 eclipse and roughly five years before the dome model began listing this as a confirmed prediction.' Open as a fast-track patch — this is a single-token fix and the longer it sits in production the more screen-readers will index it.

holes_found.description: **c3 advocate-mode escape only partially neutralized.** The c3 review identified a defense rated 3/5: 'the dome is a two-component model where surface-coupled instruments report Δg and vault-isolated SGs report 0 because the vault filters the gravitational mode while leaving the magnetic anomaly unaffected.' c3 recommended adding a sentence about the absence of a 'vault-isolation threshold, coupling-mode partition function, or any quantitative rule for which instruments at which sites should report Δg vs Δg = 0.' The actual ISS-1528 patch addresses only the subsurface-conductivity flavor of the location-dependent escape ('basement granite at Mohe vs. Devonian carbonate at Membach'). A defender can pivot from subsurface-conductivity to instrumental-mode (vault isolation, sensor type, integration window) and the patched text does not preempt that pivot. The phrase 'no per-site coupling tensor' is ambiguous enough to be read as not-quite-addressing the two-mode framing.
holes_found.recommendation: Extend the preemptive sentence to cover the broader two-mode framing: 'Nor does the model publish a vault-isolation threshold, an instrumental coupling-mode partition, or any quantitative rule for which sensor types at which sites should report Δg vs Δg = 0. Any such partition is constructed after Mohe and Membach were observed — it is a curve fit to two data points and contains no advance prediction for a third site.' This costs ~50 words and closes the c3 advocate-mode escape fully rather than partially. Recommend opening a single-issue patch on top of the existing trilemma block.

holes_found.description: **Trilemma horns labeled (a)/(b)/(c) but only (c) is independent.** Reading the three horns: (a) 'if electromagnetic: spring gravimeters shouldn't pick up EM yet WIN-011 reports a positive,' (b) 'if electromagnetic: κ in WIN-012 has zero in the denominator,' (c) 'if location-dependent: no advance specification.' Horns (a) and (b) are both consequences of the same EM interpretation — a dilemma branch with two costs, not two independent horns. A defender reading this carefully can argue 'you've stacked two consequences of one branch and called it three horns to make the rhetoric look stronger.' This is a structural rather than factual hole — the argument is fine, but the label 'three-way contradiction' / 'each horn of this trilemma' overstates the independence of the horns. Note: c3 itself flagged the same structure, but the patch did not adjust the framing.
holes_found.recommendation: Either (a) reframe as 'a two-pronged dilemma: the dome's null is either electromagnetic (which costs the model both WIN-011's positive detection AND WIN-012's κ definition) or location-dependent (which costs the model its falsifiability),' or (b) elevate horn (b) to a fully independent argument by adding a sentence explaining why EM-zeros-Δg is a separate problem from EM-shouldn't-show-on-gravimeter (e.g., one is about instrument response, the other is about coupling-constant arithmetic). Defer to a future tightening pass — not blocking.

holes_found.description: **Nested-parenthetical readability.** The Van Camp parenthetical now contains a parenthetical-within-parenthetical: '(Van Camp et al., 2001 — the dome cites \'Van Camp 1999\' (the eclipse year); the paper appeared in 2001, ...)'. The inner parenthetical 'the eclipse year' breaks reading flow and partially duplicates the outer 'paper appeared in 2001' with a different anchor year. A reader has to parse three temporal references (paper 2001, dome's 1999, eclipse 1999) packed into one bracketed aside.
holes_found.recommendation: Restructure as a free-standing sentence after the citation: 'Van Camp et al., 2001 — the dome's 02-wins.txt cites this as Van Camp 1999, conflating the eclipse year with the publication year; the paper was published two years after the eclipse.' This separates the citation from the year-discrepancy commentary and removes the nested brackets.

holes_found.description: **TLDR-evidence rhetorically grants WIN-011 a positive detection without qualification.** The tldr_evidence reads 'detecting a gravity signal (WIN-011, spring gravimeter)' as a flat-statement, but our own WIN-011 verdict is 'Not Demonstrated' (we contest the positive detection). Inside the unfalsifiability argument it is fine to grant the dome's framing rhetorically — but a TLDR is read in isolation. A reader who skims only TLDRs will see WIN-011 'detection' confidently asserted here and 'Not Demonstrated' on WIN-011's own panel. c3 noted this in cross_win_consistency but did not flag it as a hole; reconsidering at TLDR level it crosses into 'minor TLDR factual imprecision' under the dispatcher rule that TLDR factual errors are at least minor.
holes_found.recommendation: Soften the TLDR clause to: 'The dome claims credit for both an alleged gravity signal (WIN-011, spring gravimeter — disputed) and a confirmed null (WIN-013, superconducting gravimeter at Membach).' Adds three words; preserves the unfalsifiability frame; removes the cross-panel inconsistency.

stronger_arguments.description: Yang & Wang (2002) reanalysis of Mohe data — already cited in WIN-011 — attributes the 'positive' detection to instrumental drift correlated with environmental temperature. Bringing this reanalysis into WIN-013 (one sentence: 'In context: Yang & Wang 2002, cited under WIN-011, traced the Mohe anomaly to environmental drift; the Membach null is therefore not just more precise, it is the result the corrected Mohe data already implies.') closes the loop more tightly than 'SGs are more sensitive.' c3 already flagged this; not yet integrated.
sources: Yang & Wang (2002) reanalysis cited in WIN-011 detail_evidence

stronger_arguments.description: The deep-vault EM screening argument can be turned against the dome's own EM interpretation. Membach is a deep underground vault; standard physics predicts EM screening attenuates external fields. A dome defender invoking 'EM, not gravitational' to explain the Membach null is unwittingly conceding the standard-physics screening mechanism — which itself predicts a null without invoking dome geometry. We can use the dome's preferred mechanism against it: under EM, the dome's interpretation explains nothing the standard model doesn't.
sources: Hampel et al. 2003 (Membach vault EM specs) — proposed in c3, still unverified.

kernel_of_truth.description: Membach SGs really did record a null during the August 1999 eclipse, and SG nulls really are an empirical constraint on Allais-effect-style claims. Citing Van Camp et al. 2001 is not fabrication; the paper exists and the result is real. The dome's mistake is interpreting a null result that exactly matches standard physics's prediction as confirmation of a model that proposes an eclipse-induced effect.
kernel_of_truth.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: A null result that is exactly what standard physics predicts cannot be evidence FOR a model that proposes an eclipse-induced effect. The dome's escape — 'electromagnetic, not gravitational' — is a re-framing that contradicts WIN-011 (where the same eclipse phenomenon IS gravitational), zeros out WIN-012's κ, or — if location-dependent — concedes unfalsifiability. The kernel of truth (Membach null is real) leaves the dome with three options that all break other WINs.

cross_win_consistency.notes: The four-WIN cluster is internally consistent post-c3: WIN-011 (Not Demonstrated, monitoring=hardcoded), WIN-012 (Self-Contradicted, monitoring=hardcoded — explicitly cites the κ-vanishes argument), WIN-013 (Misleading, monitoring=none), WIN-014 (Misleading, monitoring=none). The argumentative structure shared between WIN-013 and WIN-014 is appropriate for parallel WINs (same logic, swapped site details). One small tension persists: WIN-013's 'WIN-011 positive detection' framing in finding/tldr is a rhetorical concession used to drive the unfalsifiability argument, while WIN-011's own panel disputes that detection. c3 noted this and judged it acceptable inside the trilemma; the c4 minor hole above re-flags it at the TLDR layer.

claims_checked: Eclipse year vs. publication year arithmetic: 2001 - 1999 = 2 years (NOT 5 — patched text in detail_evidence is wrong)

Paper-to-dome-model interval: 2001 to ~2006 ≈ 5 years (verdict_text usage 'five years before the dome model existed' is consistent with this — the math is right in verdict_text, wrong in detail_evidence)
κ = 10.9 nT / 6.5 μGal = 1.6769 nT/μGal — matches dome's stated 1.67 nT/μGal to 0.4% (re-verified via python: 10.9/6.5 = 1.6769)

Sensitivity ratio bounds: SG short-term noise floor ~0.01 μGal; LR-D spring gravimeter noise ~1–5 μGal short-term-to-drift. Min ratio 1/0.01 = 100×; max ratio 5/0.01 = 500×. The patched '100–500×' range is exactly bounded by published noise floors and is defensible.
DOI 10.1023/A:1013940928637 — prefix 10.1023 is Kluwer/Springer (consistent with Journal of Geodesy publication context); confirmed in c3 and not re-touched by patches.
quantitative_verification.computations: python3 spot-checks: 2001-1999=2; 10.9/6.5=1.6769; sensitivity ratios 1/0.01=100 and 5/0.01=500 — confirms the published-noise-floor anchor of the 100-500× claim. The single math error (5 vs 2 years) is the only computational discrepancy.
quantitative_verification.discrepancies: detail_evidence parenthetical states 'the paper appeared in 2001, five years after the eclipse' — should read 'two years after the eclipse.' This is the major-severity hole above. All other numerical claims check out cleanly post-c3 patches.

advocate_mode.best_defense: A fair-minded dome defender accepting our six methodological principles would say: 'Your trilemma assumes a single-mode interpretation — that the eclipse signal is either gravitational OR electromagnetic OR location-dependent. The dome model is in fact a two-field model: every eclipse phenomenon has both a gravitational and an electromagnetic component, with site- and instrument-specific coupling. WIN-011's spring gravimeter at Mohe sits at the surface and couples primarily to the gravitational mode, hence reports Δg ≠ 0. WIN-013's SGs at Membach sit in a deep vault that mechanically isolates the gravitational mode (the SG springs are environmentally isolated to ~0.01 μGal, but vault-mounted SGs are explicitly designed to suppress surface-coupled gravity gradients) while leaving the EM-coupled response — which in this case is also zero, because the eclipse-EM coupling is small at high latitude — unaffected. WIN-012's κ measures the conversion between the two modes and applies only to instruments that couple to both. Your trilemma collapses two-component coupling into single-mode horns and then notes inconsistency; the dome model never claimed single-mode behavior. The unfalsifiability charge therefore presupposes a model architecture the dome explicitly does not endorse.' This is a stronger version of the c3 defense (rated 3) because it speaks the language of physical instrumentation rather than vague site-dependence.
advocate_mode.preemptive_recommendation: The hole above (#2 moderate) recommends extending the preemptive sentence to cover instrumental-mode coupling explicitly. The patch ISS-1528 only addressed subsurface-conductivity, which is the easiest flavor to refute. The harder flavor — vault-isolation, sensor-type partition — needs explicit treatment. Recommended sentence after the existing subsurface-conductivity preemptive: 'Nor does the model publish a vault-isolation threshold, an instrumental coupling-mode partition, or any quantitative rule for which sensor types at which sites should report Δg vs Δg = 0. Any such partition would have to be constructed after Mohe and Membach were observed — a curve fit to two data points with no third-site prediction.' This converts the strongest two-mode defense into a documented absence in the dome's published equations and timestamps.

deeper_analysis: The c3 hardening was three-quarters successful. Three of the four ISS patches landed cleanly: ISS-1527 (sensitivity range bounds), ISS-1528 (subsurface-conductivity preemptive — partial coverage of the c3 advocate-mode escape), and ISS-1530 (specialized post-hoc paragraph). The fourth, ISS-1529, intended to surface the Van Camp 1999/2001 year discrepancy — and did so successfully — but introduced a new arithmetic error ('five years after the eclipse' instead of 'two') that needs reverting. The verdict (Misleading) holds; the trilemma is sound; the cross-WIN consistency is intact; the κ math is verified to 0.4%. Recommended action stays at minor_edit, with one major-severity single-token revert needed urgently and one moderate-severity advocate-mode tightening recommended for the next pass. The pattern this run reveals: a c3 review with multiple accepted recommendations can produce patches that each land but introduce small new errors at the edges. The c-cycle pattern is doing what it should — c4 catching the c3-patch artifact.

citations_verified: Wang et al. 2000, Phys. Rev. D 62 041101, DOI 10.1103/PhysRevD.62.041101 — Mohe spring gravimeter (verified c2/c3, unchanged in c4 content).
Van Camp et al. 2001, DOI 10.1023/A:1013940928637 — Membach SG null (DOI prefix 10.1023 = Kluwer/Springer; year 2001 correct; eclipse 1999 correct).
citations_unchecked: Hampel et al. 2003 (Membach vault EM specs) — still proposed in stronger_arguments, still unverified. c3 noted it; no patch attempted to cite it.
Yang & Wang (2002) Mohe reanalysis — proposed strengthening, still not in WIN-013 text but exists in WIN-011's detail_evidence per cross-reference. c3 flagged; no patch attempted.
